did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses a deep learning framework named DermFusion-X, which is a hybrid architecture combining a CNN branch (modified ResNet50) and a Vision Transformer branch (ViT-Base/16) with a cross-attention fusion block. This AI model was trained and evaluated to perform skin cancer classification on the HAM10000 and ISIC 2020 datasets, achieving 94.0% accuracy.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work introducing a novel deep learning framework named DermFusion-X. It details the proposed architecture (hybrid CNN-ViT with cross-attention), training methodology (focal loss, augmentation), and reports new empirical results (94.0% accuracy, 91.0% F1) on the HAM10000 and ISIC 2020 datasets. Although published in conference proceedings (ICECA-2025), it contains original technical contributions and validation rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: Deep Learning–Driven Multimodal Framework for Accurate Skin Cancer Classification
journal: Proceedings of the 9th International Conference on Electronics, Communication and Aerospace Technology (ICECA-2025)
brief_summary: This study introduces DermFusion-X, a deep learning framework for skin cancer classification that combines a modified ResNet50 CNN branch with a Vision Transformer branch via a cross-attention fusion block. The model was trained and evaluated on the HAM10000 and ISIC 2020 datasets to address class imbalance and generalization challenges in dermatology. Key methodological innovations include the use of focal loss with gamma 2.0 and cosine annealing with warm restarts to stabilize minority-class learning. The proposed framework achieved 94.0% accuracy, 91.0% precision, 90.0% recall, and 91.0% F1-score, significantly outperforming baseline models like CNN+SVM and standard ViT architectures. These results demonstrate that principled fusion and imbalance-aware training are effective for achieving robust and interpretable skin cancer diagnosis.

all_task_types.target: Seven discrete skin lesion classes (Melanoma, Nevus, Basal Cell Carcinoma, Actinic Keratosis, Seborrheic Keratosis, Dermatofibroma, Vascular lesions)
all_task_types.details: The paper explicitly describes the task as 'skin cancer classification' and 'dermoscopic classification'. The model outputs a softmax probability distribution over seven lesion classes using a classifier MLP. Performance is evaluated using accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score, and AUC, which are standard metrics for multi-class classification tasks.

datasets_used.name: HAM10000
datasets_used.description: A large collection of multi-source dermatoscopic images of common pigmented skin lesions, referenced as a public dataset used in the study.

use.testing: Used for testing the DermFusion-X model.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2020
datasets_used.description: A public dataset from the International Skin Imaging Collaboration, used alongside HAM10000 for evaluation.

use.training: Used for training the DermFusion-X model.
use.validation: Used for validation of the DermFusion-X model.
use.testing: Used for testing the DermFusion-X model.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance comparisons were made using descriptive metrics (accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score, AUC) without statistical significance testing.

HAM10000_and_ISIC_2020.metric: Balanced Accuracy, Precision, Recall, Macro F1-Score, AUC, Inference Time
HAM10000_and_ISIC_2020.notes: DermFusion-X achieved a balanced accuracy of 86.7% and macro F1-score of 0.84. Notable improvements were observed in underrepresented classes such as Dermatofibroma (DF) and Vascular lesions (VASC). The model outperformed baselines including CNN+SVM (40% accuracy) and standard ViT/ResNet architectures. The paper notes that DermFusion-X approaches recent ViT- and GNN-based state-of-the-art results.

architecture_used.main_model: DermFusion-X (hybrid CNN-Vision Transformer with cross-attention fusion)
architecture_used.pretraining: Not explicitly reported; context mentions 'transfer-learned CNNs' as a general practice and 'fine-tunes a pretrained backbone' for baselines, but does not specify pretraining weights for the proposed DermFusion-X components.

branches.output: 196 patch tokens plus a CLS token

conclusions: The proposed DermFusion-X framework, a hybrid CNN-Vision Transformer architecture with cross-attention fusion, achieves 94.0% accuracy, 91.0% precision, 90.0% recall, and 91.0% F1-score on the HAM10000 and ISIC 2020 datasets.
DermFusion-X significantly outperforms the CNN+SVM baseline (40% accuracy) and approaches the performance of recent ViT- and GNN-based state-of-the-art models.

Cross-attentive fusion combined with focal-loss scheduling (gamma=2.0) and cosine annealing with warm restarts yields strong, balanced performance by effectively addressing class imbalance and stabilizing minority-class learning.
The model provides interpretable attention maps that align with dermatological diagnostic cues, offering a clinically practical path between efficiency and accuracy.

The proposed design demonstrates that principled fusion mechanisms and imbalance-aware training are decisive for dermatology AI, offering a pragmatic alternative to computationally intensive foundation models.
DermFusion-X is suitable for deployment on mobile or edge devices due to its balanced precision and computational cost, avoiding the need for large-scale foundation model compute.

The study acknowledges that while the model improves generalization through advanced augmentation (RandAugment, Mixup, CutMix) and regularization, challenges such as domain shift across devices and skin tones remain relevant for real-world clinical adoption.
important_remarks: The study acknowledges that challenges such as domain shift across devices and skin tones remain relevant for real-world clinical adoption, indicating limited external validation beyond the HAM10000 and ISIC 2020 datasets.
The paper does not report formal statistical hypothesis testing, p-values, or confidence intervals; performance comparisons rely on descriptive metrics without statistical significance testing.

The proposed DermFusion-X model achieves a balanced accuracy of 86.7% and macro F1-score of 0.84, which is lower than the reported accuracy of 94.0% for a standard Vision Transformer baseline, suggesting potential overfitting or dataset-specific bias in the proposed model's evaluation.

The paper notes that the baseline CNN+SVM approach achieved only 40% accuracy on ISIC, highlighting the severity of class imbalance and the difficulty of the task, but does not provide detailed per-class breakdowns for the baseline to contextualize the improvement.

The model's performance on underrepresented classes (Dermatofibroma and Vascular lesions) is noted as improved, but specific metrics for these minority classes are not provided in the extracted results, limiting assessment of its claimed advantage in handling class imbalance.
The study is published in conference proceedings (ICECA-2025) rather than a peer-reviewed journal, which may imply less rigorous scrutiny compared to journal publications.
